Chapter 3 Data Visualisation Data visualisation plays a crucial role in both ex- and post- data analysis. Prior to data analysis, it helps organise complex and unstructured data into statistically meaningful graphical representations, enabling researchers to explore patterns, identify trends, and detect potential relationships within the data. After the analysis, visualisation serves as an effective medium for communicating results, helping to convey the key findings and maintain a coherent narrative throughout the analytical process. This chapter introduces data visualisation techniques in the R programming language, with particular emphasis on the use of the ggplot2 package. Various types of charts and their corresponding coding approaches are presented, along with detailed explanations of graphical customisation options that allow figures to be refined and tailored for clearer and more informative presentation. The ggplot2 package is one of the most widely used visualisation tools in R. The term “gg” refers to the Grammar of Graphics, indicating that plots generated with this package are constructed according to a systematic set of rules, analogous to the grammatical structure of a language. The ggplot2 package provides a wide range of functions for producing different types of charts, while also offering extensive flexibility to adjust graphical elements and styles, thereby improving the clarity and interpretability of the data. In ggplot2, the plotting framework is primarily composed of four fundamental components: data and aesthetic mappings (axes and variables), geometric objects (chart types), scale settings, and theme settings. The overall structure of the plotting framework is illustrated in Figure 3.1. Figure 3.1: Structure of plot using ggplot2 Taking Figure 3.2 as an example, the graphical elements controlled by each component are summarised as follows: Data and axes: The input dataset is the iris dataset, with sepal length (Sepal.Length) specified as the horizontal axis and sepal width (Sepal.Width) specified as the vertical axis. Geometric object: A scatter plot is used. Scale settings: Petal width (Petal.Width) is mapped to a colour gradient, and petal length (Petal.Length) is mapped to the size of the points. Theme settings: The colour and size of the axis text are customised, and the legend text size, weight, and background colour are specified. Figure 3.2: Example of plotting using ggplot2 package This chapter introduces commonly used chart types and systematically summarises the core plotting concepts of the ggplot2 package. However, it is not possible to cover all chart types or the full range of graphical customisation options. Readers are therefore encouraged to search for relevant resources online using appropriate keywords. By examining and adapting example code from others, one can effectively learn additional plotting techniques and improve their visualisation skills. In addition, the following resources provide rich galleries of R visualisations, many of which include complete code examples that can serve as useful references for learning and implementation: The R Graph Gallery R CHARTS from Data to Viz Visual Vocabulary 3.1 Chart Types Summary of Chart Types This section summarises commonly used chart types (as shown in Table ??) and introduces the code executed to create each type of chart. Through different forms of data visualisation, various characteristics and patterns within the data can be effectively presented.
